calibration_nodes.text: What type of electronic games do you usually play or like the most?
What style of music do you most often listen to with headphones?

options.text: I prefer silence and rarely listen to music

calibration_nodes.text: During offline social gatherings, which activities do you prefer?

options.text: I avoid pointless socializing and prefer being alone

calibration_nodes.text: Besides football, what physical exercise do you tend to prefer?
In daily life, what kind of pet would you prefer to keep you company?

question_pool.text: I am more easily moved by things with history, heritage, and shared memory than by newly fashionable concepts.
If two brands are similar in quality, I trust the one tested by time more.
When joining a group, I care whether it has clear traditions, rituals, and identity.
I appreciate designs with classical order, weight, and continuity.
The fact that a group has lasted for a long time already proves it has inner value worth respecting.
I would rather slowly enter a mature and reliable path than constantly chase brand-new opportunities.
I am usually cautious toward the urge to overthrow old rules and reinvent everything.
I want to belong to something larger than my present individual choices.
I prefer organizations with stable systems, clear hierarchy, and transferable experience.
I respect people who hold one set of values for a long time, even if they seem conservative.
If something new is exciting enough, I can quickly abandon old habits and old identities.
History and heritage are often baggage; what really matters is who is stronger right now.
At a party with strangers, I am usually the one who actively finds topics and controls the rhythm.
When disagreement appears, I tend to state my view first and pull the situation into my rhythm.
When the task direction is unclear, I actively propose a plan instead of waiting for others.
I prefer striking first and using constant pressure to make the opponent adapt to me.
Facing a new field, I prefer trial, output, and feedback over watching from the side for a long time.
I dislike only reacting; I would rather be the person who pushes the situation forward.
I actively set topics, boundaries, and rhythm to avoid being led by others.
When a good chance appears, I would rather rush in and occupy the position first, then fix details later.
If I hold a strong view, I usually say it directly instead of circling around for a long time.
The more stuck a situation becomes, the more I want to actively break the balance.
In uncertain situations, I prefer stabilizing the defense first and waiting for the other side to show a weakness.
I usually do not rush to lead the conversation; I prefer observing how others unfold it.
At crucial moments, I believe one top person's flash of brilliance can change the whole direction.
If the team is in trouble, I hope I can be the one who steps up and solves it.
I am more easily fired up by scenes where one protagonist turns the tide alone.
I especially admire people with irreplaceable individual ability.
When everyone hesitates, I am willing to take higher risks to create a breakthrough.
Great leaders should have decision-making power beyond the system at key moments.
I enjoy being trusted in important moments and answering that expectation with performance.
The harder and more unwanted a problem is, the more it triggers my fighting spirit.
Sometimes one person's ceiling decides the final height more than team process does.
I do not mind being the focus if it means I can influence the result.
I trust stable division of labor and collective execution more than waiting for one person to explode.
A mature team should reduce its dependence on star individuals as much as possible.
As long as the goal is achieved, using less graceful or opportunistic methods is acceptable if they are not illegal.
I believe reaching the goal matters more than whether the process looks elegant.
If resources are limited, I prioritize the places most likely to create real returns.
When making major decisions, I try to lower emotion and calculate real gains and losses.
I care more about whether something can truly land than whether it feels idealistic.
If a solution is effective enough, I do not mind if it looks clumsy or unattractive.
On major interest issues, I prefer making rules and accounts clear.
When necessary, I can give up a pretty posture and choose the most direct effective solution.
I respect people who get things done, even if they are not romantic.
If I must choose, I protect the result first, then discuss style and sentiment.
I am willing to accept obvious real-world loss for pure passion.
If the process violates my aesthetics and principles, even a good result is hard for me to accept.
When traveling, I plan every day down to the hour; I dislike go-with-the-flow trips full of unknowns.
Before starting a complex task, I first break down structure, steps, and risks.
I like organizing scattered information into a clear framework before judging further.
I believe stable rhythm and clear roles help teams perform better.
I gain security from order, such as lists, schedules, and fixed processes.
When explaining complex problems, I start from background and logical chains.
I trust preparation and reviewable methods more than improvisation.
I dislike things constantly changing direction and interrupting the original plan.
I prefer progressing through a systematic course rather than learning bits and pieces everywhere.
When the surroundings are chaotic, my first reaction is to establish order.
I often feel plans limit creativity; doing and adjusting suits me better.
Facing a new problem, I like jumping in first and summarizing patterns later.
The worse the start, the more it awakens my unwillingness to lose.
After a failure, I usually enter review and counterattack mode quickly.
When questioned, I do not retreat immediately; I want even more to prove myself.
If the goal matters, I can endure a long low period.
Falling behind does not make me give up quickly; it makes me more focused.
I can keep pushing while uncomfortable instead of waiting until I fully recover.
The harder the situation, the clearer it becomes whether I truly want to win.
When morale drops, I am willing to keep holding the situation and stop it from collapsing.
I am not very afraid of being dragged into a war of attrition because I believe I can endure it.
If I truly care about something, short-term setbacks rarely make me walk away.
Once the situation clearly goes wrong, I tend to cut losses quickly and preserve energy.
After several failures in a row, I quickly doubt whether I should continue.
If I must choose a PE class, I would pick boxing or weightlifting over fencing or gymnastics.
When facing a stuck obstacle, I first try a more direct and forceful breakthrough.
I appreciate the intimidation created by speed, power, and pressure.
When necessary, I do not mind using a tough stance to make others feel my boundaries.
I trust execution, physical energy, and repeated impact more than delicate calculation.
I respect people who can absorb pressure and stand at the very front.
In matches or action scenes, I am easily attracted by direct physical confrontation.
High-intensity, fast-paced tasks that require hard resistance sometimes excite me.
I believe physical condition and action energy deeply influence a person's judgment.
When a situation freezes, I tend to increase force rather than take a detour first.
I admire clever handling that uses angles and skill more than charging straight ahead.
If skill and leverage can solve a problem, I usually do not want to spend energy forcing it.
When a half-executed plan is suddenly scrapped, I can stop complaining and switch quickly.
When a plan suddenly fails, I usually start looking for a new way to play very quickly.
Facing different circles, I can naturally adjust my expression and rhythm.
When new rules appear, I get excited to study the opportunities they may bring.
I do not reject temporary direction changes if they open new space.
I am willing to try unfamiliar cities, foods, and routes.
If the original method proves unsuitable, I can let it go quickly without obsessing over sunk cost.
I can switch among roles such as organizer, executor, and observer.
I often recombine limited resources at hand into a new solution.
The unknown does not always make me anxious; sometimes it makes things feel alive.
Once I decide a method works, I prefer continuing to use it even when the environment changes.
If familiar rules are broken, I usually need a long time to get back into rhythm.
I naturally like old champions or heroes that have survived many patches and still have loyal players.
I believe that if you master one signature hero to an exceptional level, you can still make it work even when it is not meta.
In the early game, whenever I see a chance, I actively pressure lane, invade, roam, or support teammates to create tempo.
I prefer engaging first or forcing enemy cooldowns instead of waiting for opponents to make mistakes.
I am deeply attracted to heroes that can change a decisive teamfight with one perfect sequence.
If the game is stuck, I would rather believe I can open the situation through one perfect play.
As long as it helps me win, I can accept picking heroes and playstyles that are not flashy but have higher win rates.
Even if the process feels frustrating, I can accept avoiding fights, trading resources, and farming quietly if it leads to victory.
I care a lot about waves, vision, jungle resources, and key neutral objective timers; I like holding the map rhythm in my hands.
Compared with constant fighting, I enjoy slowly expanding an advantage through macro, vision, and resource trades.
Even if the early game collapses, as long as the base is standing, I seriously look for comeback chances.
When teammates become negative or start surrender votes, I want even more to stabilize the game and drag it later.
I prefer tanks, fighters, or heroes that can charge directly into the enemy formation, absorb damage, and tear it open.
Compared with kiting and poking, I prefer fighting face-to-face and crushing opponents through raw pressure.
After a patch update, I am willing to quickly try new heroes, builds, and playstyles instead of sticking to old habits.
If the shape of a game suddenly changes, I can quickly adjust my build, lane assignment, or teamfight plan.
I have a natural preference for classic maps like Dust2 and Inferno—new maps just don’t feel like they have the same soul.
I’d rather hold a standard position I’ve played a thousand times than try an off-angle I’ve never practiced, just for the element of surprise.
In a firefight, I like to peek first to gather intel and look for the opening pick.
When the economy allows it, I prefer to rush at full speed, using tempo and surprise to disrupt the opponent’s setup.
In a 1vN clutch situation, I don’t get nervous—I get excited. That’s the best stage to prove individual skill.
In-game, when the team is struggling and morale is low, I’ll step up to make the call, break through, and swing the momentum with my own rhythm.
On an eco round with low HP, I won’t gamble for a miracle—I’ll save my weapon to build economy for the next round.
As long as we win, I’m fine doing the dirty work all game—entry fragging, holding the rear, covering flanks. My stats don’t matter.
More than direct gunfights, I enjoy the satisfaction of carving up map space step by step with smokes, molotovs, and flashes.
On the defensive side, I prefer to hold a key angle patiently, waiting for the opponent to make a mistake, rather than pushing forward to create opportunities.
In competitive shooters, even if we’re far behind at halftime, I’ll still play every round seriously after the side switch—there are too many comeback stories.
When we’re on a losing streak with a broken economy, I actually calm down and organize the team for a solid eco round, waiting for the comeback window.
In-game, I have absolute confidence in my aim—even if I’m in a bad position, I’ll hard-peek and bet on my accuracy.
When I have the AWP, I prefer to play aggressively, scoping in and pushing forward to challenge, rather than sitting deep holding a pixel angle.
In-game, if we can’t break through from one direction, I’ll quickly switch angles and routes instead of banging our heads against their defense.
When our standard play gets countered, I can quickly switch to off-angle positions or off-meta weapons to catch them off guard.
When I enter champ select, I'm already simulating the full team comp's power curve in my head—when we should spike, where the enemy will slip.
In the laning phase, I excel at denying—precisely disrupting the opponent's last-hit rhythm and suppressing their economy through micro-play feels more rewarding than just last-hitting.
What fascinates me most about DOTA2 is that a single hero can be played in completely different ways—the complex item tree and skill build branching are a form of intellectual strategy in themselves.
Before queuing for ranked, I proactively enable Do Not Disturb mode to block all notifications and ensure my game isn't interrupted.
When dropping, I deliberately avoid hot zones—I’d rather gear up slowly than get caught in an early brawl. Safe looting wins late-game.
As soon as the circle timer starts, I’m already planning my rotation route, cover spots, and positioning—I never wait until the zone is closing in.
In the final circle with bad terrain, I’d rather ditch fully-kitted gear and rush to claim the only cover or high ground—survival comes before firepower.
In Valorant, I’d rather pick a Controller than a Duelist—using abilities to zone off areas, cut off vision, and force enemy movement is more satisfying than pure gunplay.
In Valorant, once my ultimate is fully charged, I don’t rush to use it—I wait for the decisive moment to turn the entire round. One perfect ult is worth more than a wasted burst.
The evo-shield system pushes me to take fights early—not for the kills, but to level my armor to purple or red before the final circle.
When a teammate goes down, I don’t rush to revive—I read whether the enemy is holding the angle. Sometimes reviving is just giving them a free kill; you have to know when to cut your losses.
Long-range tap-firing and high-magnification recoil control are what I practice the most—in PUBG there are no shortcuts to good aim. One missed shot and it’s over.
I’ve gotten used to matches where I only have one or two direct gunfights—most of the game is spent looting, rotating, scouting, and waiting.
More than the flood of new IPs, I’d rather revisit old classics I’ve already beaten a dozen times—new games can rarely replace that timeless charm.
When I start a new game, I absolutely refuse to check guides or spoilers—even if I get stuck for days, the satisfaction of figuring it out myself is leagues above copying a walkthrough.
As long as I get the clear, I don’t care about style points. Chugging potions, chipping away sliver by sliver, even getting lucky with a crit—whatever works is fair game.
I can’t stand missing any hidden content—every new area gets swept clean; even bosses drive me to chase no-hit or perfect-parry runs. Leaving any item behind genuinely bothers me.
Dying forty or fifty times to the same boss is perfectly normal to me—I just make the run back every time. I refuse to believe there’s a boss I can’t beat with enough practice.
The more a boss or stage is known as a brick wall, the more I want to break through it on my own—proving to myself that I can do it is even more addictive than beating the game.
If I’ve tried multiple approaches against a boss and still can’t beat it, I won’t bash my head against the wall—I’ll step back, break down its patterns, re-gear, and come back with a hard counter.

Even after investing months into a character, I’ll drop them without hesitation if the meta shifts and their numbers get countered—sunk cost doesn’t get a say in my team-building. That’s the fundamental difference between gacha games and single-player ones.
In Elden Ring, if I can’t beat something, I’ll just mount up and ride elsewhere to level and gear up, then come back to crush it—that’s the whole point of an open world.

To me, Sekiro is the purest test of action skill in the Souls lineage—no builds, no spirit ashes, no overleveling. If you can’t beat it, you just can’t. All you can do is keep practicing until your muscles remember every frame.
In God of War, I never hang back and throw the axe—I rush in for brutal melee, parry into runic attacks, pop Spartan Rage, and bulldoze everything. Kratos’s combat is meant to be a steamroll.
In Red Dead Redemption 2, I never rush the story—I stop for every random encounter, brew a coffee at camp, and just watch the sunset on horseback. This game was never meant to be sprinted through.

When listening to music, I firmly believe an album is a complete work—from the intro through every track to the outro, the sequencing, the emotional arc, it’s all intentional. Skipping tracks or shuffling ruins it.

I never rely on algorithms or trending charts to discover new music—I browse niche forums, dig through obscure labels, and trace samples back to the original tracks. Passive recommendations hold zero appeal for me.
In metal, only black metal, death metal, and doom metal are the real deal with true soul—bands that borrow metal elements but are essentially alt-rock or core don’t even deserve to be called metal.
Led Zeppelin, Pink Floyd, and Black Sabbath in the 70s defined the ceiling of rock music—no matter how much later rock evolves, it will never surpass the foundation laid during that golden era.
When I listen to jazz, I fixate on the complexity of the improvisation—the chord substitutions in a bebop solo or the odd-meter rhythmic shifts excite me more than the melody itself.

I collect multiple recordings of the same piece by different conductors and orchestras and compare them endlessly—Karajan’s precision, Bernstein’s passion, Gould’s rebelliousness, each one tells a completely different story.

I have zero interest in mainstream EDM and commercial electronic music—what’s actually worth listening to is buried in Berlin’s underground clubs, Detroit’s old warehouse parties, and Bandcamp pages with only a few hundred followers.

K-POP is about more than just the music—it’s fan culture. During comeback season, I stream to boost charts, rally sales, defend ratings, and fight anti-fans. Pushing my idol’s achievements to the top is where the real engagement lies.
What I value most in a rapper is street credibility in their lyrics—you can tell instantly whether the story is fabricated or genuinely grew out of the struggle.

The beauty of the blues is that it was already complete 100 years ago—what Robert Johnson, Muddy Waters, and B.B. King created remains the pure form; the white blues-rock that came after is just a diluted version.

Pop music getting looked down on is nothing new, but I don’t think listening to mainstream pop makes anyone inferior—a song that resonates with tens of millions of people is an achievement in its own right.

I firmly believe classic training methods will never go out of style—running is running, lifting is lifting. All those flashy new training systems and smart devices are just repackaging the same principles.

I don’t need a coach or a preset program to tell me what to do during training—I research movement patterns on my own, record myself for form checks, and study sports science articles. Designing my own plan is far more effective than passively following one.

In sports, individual achievement feels far more rewarding than team success—finishing a marathon alone, pulling a heavy deadlift, or summiting a peak with nothing but your own strength, that sense of self-reliance is irreplaceable.
I have zero obsession with workout gear or ritual—a worn-out pair of running shoes and shorts and I’m out the door. Exercise is about getting the work done, not putting on a show.

I never block out dedicated gym time—I walk instead of driving short distances, take the stairs instead of the elevator. Weaving movement into daily life is far more sustainable than scheduled training.
I am borderline obsessive about tracking training data and nutrition—daily calorie intake down to the gram, every set and rep logged. Training without data to back it up is just guessing.

On the last set when I’m already at failure, I still force myself to grind out one more rep—even if my form starts breaking down and every muscle fiber is screaming at me to stop. Skipping those extra reps makes the whole session feel incomplete.

I genuinely enjoy the physical confrontation in sports—muscle-on-muscle contact when boxing out, the suffocating burn in your lungs during a full sprint, the searing ache of muscles pushed to their limit. That raw physical feedback is the very essence of sport.

I refuse to lock myself into a single training modality—when my strength peaks, I add cardio; when flexibility becomes a weakness, I pick up yoga; when I hit a plateau, I switch the entire movement pattern. The body needs diverse stimulus to keep progressing.

I strictly split my training into bulking, cutting, and peaking phases—caloric surplus, training volume, and exercise selection all get re-planned for each cycle. Training without periodization is just mindlessly stacking fatigue.

In doubles, when the opponents keep pinning me to the backcourt, I won’t just keep grinding high clears—I drop to the net, switch pacing with flat drives, or fire body shots to break their positioning. Breaking through with tactical shifts is far smarter than brute stamina.

On the court I naturally gravitate toward the paint—boxing out on rebounds, seeking contact on drives, using my torso to impose on defense. Overpowering opponents with physical strength satisfies me way more than scoring from outside.

When I hit the wall during a long run, I actually get more fired up—my breathing falls apart, my legs feel like concrete, my head fills with every excuse to stop, but it’s precisely in that state that every extra step I push through defines what running truly means to me.

Yoga for me is not about relaxation—I spend entire sessions refining the micro-alignment of an inversion or arm balance: the firing sequence of my core, the position of my shoulder blades, the synchronization of breath with movement. If it’s not precise, I keep drilling.

When climbing, I spend just as much time on the ground as on the wall—before I even touch a hold, I run through the entire route in my head multiple times: how to grip each hold, where to shift my center of gravity, which spots let me hang and rest. Planning everything out before pulling on is twice as efficient as just throwing myself at it.

Choosing the restaurant for every group dinner is my domain—I’m the first to scout new openings, I know every hidden alley gem like the back of my hand. Cuisine, budget, per-head cost—I lock it in within five minutes, and my friends never have a bad meal with me.

At karaoke, I am the atmosphere engine for the entire room—I seize control of the playlist from the first song, ballads, rock, shout-along anthems, I’ll take them all. I only surrender the mic when my voice is raw. A room without me is just background instrumentals.

Drinking, for me, is about atmosphere and conversation—I can’t sit still in loud nightclubs or rowdy food stalls. I gravitate toward quiet craft beer bars, whisky lounges, or izakayas where you can actually talk. What I’m drinking for has never been the alcohol percentage.

I’m the type who dances to the very last song and only leaves when the lights come on—my friends drop off one by one, and I’m still soaked in the middle of the floor. Walking out drenched is the proof that tonight was worth the ticket.

When I go clubbing, I need to be in the dead center of the dance floor—lights on my face, bass pounding through my chest. I don’t care if I look good or not; when the vibe is right, the entire floor is my stage.

Most people’s first reaction to snakes, spiders, or lizards is fear and rejection, but I see a beauty in them that’s obscured by prejudice—being willing to understand and accept these misunderstood creatures is itself an aesthetic judgment that refuses to follow the crowd.
Raising cats or dogs is beneath me — I close my eyes and nurture my spirit instead.
